summary: Jeff Atwood shares his essential laptop travel accessories, focusing on practical solutions to common mobile computing frustrations. He recommends Microsoft's wireless mini-mouse for its ability to track on shiny surfaces and clever power-saving design. The post highlights the importance of external USB WiFi adapters with antennas for getting reliable signals in areas with weak coverage. He also advocates for retractable cables to minimize cable clutter while traveling. The piece reflects the practical hardware challenges of mobile computing in the mid-2000s era.
key_points: Built-in laptop pointing devices (touchpads, nubs) are inadequate for precise work - a quality wireless mini-mouse is essential
Free WiFi availability is overrated outside dense urban areas; laptop internal antennas often can't get usable signals
External USB WiFi adapters with directional antennas can be positioned optimally, independent of laptop orientation
Retractable cables solve the cable management problem for travelers
Small design details matter - like Microsoft's clever receiver-as-off-switch solution
money_quotes: Touchpads and nubs are fine when there's no other option, but for quicker, more precise work, you gotta have a mouse.
In my experience, the prevalance of free WiFi is wildly overrated.
You just can't expect to get a decent WiFi signal from your laptop's puny internal antenna except in the most optimal of conditions.
It is external and can be positioned for optimal signal, e.g., facing the correct direction, which may have no relationship to the way you want to sit and use your laptop.
Screw portability, I'm tempted to carry a cantenna with me for those truly difficult to reach signal locations, like my Mom's house.
I barely leave the house enough to qualify for the Road title, much less Warrior.
key_insight: Mobile computing productivity depends heavily on having the right peripheral accessories to overcome the inherent limitations of laptop hardware.
